nameMeanings.name: Morris
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English and Scottish: from Maurice, an Old French personal name introduced to Britain by the Normans. It is derived from Latin Mauritius, a derivative of Maurus (see Moore ). This was the name of several early Christian saints. In some cases it may be a nickname of the same derivation for someone with a swarthy complexion.
Irish: Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó Muirghis, a variant of Ó Muirgheasa (see Morrissey ).

Welsh: Anglicized form of the personal name Meurig (from Latin Mauritius), which was gradually superseded in Wales by Morus, Morys, a derivative of the Anglo-Norman French form of the name (see 1 above).

variants.name: Moore
meanings: English: from Middle English more ‘moor, marsh, fen’ (Old English mōr), hence a topographic name for someone who lived in such a place, or a habitational name from any of various places called with this word, as for example Moore in Cheshire or More in Shropshire.
English (of Norman origin): ethnic name from Old French more ‘Moor’, either someone from North Africa or, more often, a nickname for someone thought to resemble a Moor. Compare Morrell and Moreau .

English (of Norman origin): from the Middle English personal name More (Old French More, Maur, Latin Maurus), originally denoting either ‘Moor’ or someone with a swarthy complexion (compare Morrell , Morrin , Morris , and sense 2 above). There was a 6th-century Christian saint of this name.

variants.name: Mauritz
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Fritz, Armin, Frieda, Helmut, Kurt, Reinhold, Wolfgang.
German and Dutch: from the personal name Mauritz, from Latin Mauritius (see Morris ).

variants.name: Mauricio
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Manuel, Juan, Carlos, Mario, Roberto, Lilia, Luis, Adela, Andres, Antero, Cipriano.  Portuguese Manoel.
Portuguese (Maurício) and Spanish: from the personal name Maurício (Portuguese), Mauricio (Spanish), derived from the Latin name Mauritius (see Morris ).

variants.name: Maurice
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Andre, Pierre, Armand, Denys, Gabrielle, Antoine, Benoit, Camille, Cecile, Francois, Gaston, Germain.
French, English, Welsh, Scottish, and Irish: from the Old French personal name Maurice, Latin Mauritius (see Morris ).

variants.name: McMorris
meanings: Scottish and Irish: Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Muiris, a patronymic from a Gaelicized form of Morris . In some cases this is a Gaelicized form of Fitzmaurice .
Scottish and Irish: Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Muirgheasa ‘son of Muirgheas’, a Gaelic personal name meaning ‘sea valor’ or ‘sea choice’. Compare Morrissey .

variants.name: Morici
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Salvatore, Antonio, Gasper, Aldo, Angelo, Annamarie, Elvio, Giuseppe, Sal, Santo, Vito.
Italian: patronymic or plural form of a variant of the personal name Maurice (see Morris ).
Hungarian: patronymic from the personal name Móric, Hungarian form of Morris .

variants.name: Moris
meanings: English: variant of   Morris  .

French, Luxembourgish, Flemish, Walloon, North German, and Dutch: from a vernacular form of the French personal name Maurice (see Morris ). In North America, the surname Moris may also be an altered form of French Maurice .
Americanized form of German   Moritz  .

variants.name: Merrick
meanings: Welsh: from the personal name Meuric, the Middle Welsh form of Maurice (see Morris ).
Irish: borrowed from Welsh. In Ireland, when this is not the Welsh name, it might be adopted for Irish Ó Mearadhaigh, see Merry 2.

variants.name: Maurizio
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Carlo, Marcello, Marco, Mario, Fernando, Matteo, Natale, Sal, Valentino, Vito.
Italian: from the personal name Maurizio, from Latin Mauritius (see Morris 1).

variants.name: Morehouse
meanings: English (Yorkshire): habitational name from any of the many places called Moorhouse in Yorkshire or Durham (from Old English mōr ‘moor’ + hūs ‘house’). In some cases the surname may have become Morris . This name is also established in Ireland.

nameMeanings.name: II
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Japanese: written 井伊 ‘well’ and ‘that’, the name one of Japan's great daimyō families, originally based in Tōtōmi (now part of Shizuoka prefecture), later in Kōzuke (now Gunma prefecture) and Ōmi (now Shiga prefecture). Other variants of the name substitute either character with similar sounding characters such as 飯 ‘cooked rice’, 居 ‘residence’, and so forth. It was formerly Romanized as Iyi. — Note: In the population figure published by the US Census Bureau, the Roman number II (meaning ‘the second’, i.e. ‘the younger’ of the two bearers of the name) is apparently also counted as a surname Ii.

History: Ii Naomasa (1561–1602) was awarded lands in Kōzuke and Ōmi for his service in the civil wars that won the shōgunate (military dictatorship) for Tokugawa Ieyasu. His family built the beautiful castle of Hikone, which still stands. His descendant Ii Naosuke (1815–1860) ruled Japan for two years as Tairō (Great Elder) and played a major part in signing treaties that opened Japan to foreign trade at the end of the Tokugawa Shōgunate. He was assassinated by adherents of the anti-foreign party.

nameMeanings.name: Edward
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: From an Old English personal name derived from ēad ‘prosperity, riches’ + weard ‘guard’. This has been one of the most successful of all Old English names, in frequent use from before the Conquest to the present day, and even being exported into other European languages. It was the name of three Anglo-Saxon kings and has been borne by eight kings of England since the Norman Conquest. It is also the name of the youngest son of Queen Elizabeth II . The most influential early bearer was King Edward the Confessor ( ?1002–66 ; ruled 1042–66 ). In a troubled period of English history, he contrived to rule fairly and (for a time at any rate) firmly. But in the latter part of his reign he paid more attention to his religion than to his kingdom. He died childless, and his death sparked off conflicting claims to his throne, which were resolved by the victory of William the Conqueror at the Battle of Hastings. His memory was honoured by Normans and English alike, for his fairness and his piety. Edward's mother was Norman; he had spent part of his youth in Normandy; and William claimed to have been nominated by Edward as his successor. Edward was canonized in the 12th century, and came to be venerated throughout Europe as a model of a Christian king.

variants.name: Edoardo
meanings: From English. See Edward .

variants.name: Eduardo
meanings: From English. See Edward .

variants.name: Eideard
meanings: Gaelic equivalent of Edward .

variants.name: Édouard
meanings: From English. See Edward .

variants.name: Edvard
meanings: form of English Edward .

historicEvents.title: State Lottery
historicEvents.description: A State Lottery was recorded in 1569. The tickets were sold at St. Paul's Cathedral in London.

historicEvents.title: The Royal Exchange is Organized
historicEvents.description: The Royal Exchange was organized in January 1571 by Sir Thomas Gresham. Gresham is known as the father of English banking.

historicEvents.title: The First English Colony is Established in America
historicEvents.description: England established its first colony in North America in 1585 as a way to generate additional wealth. The colony was named Virginia, after the virgin Queen Elizabeth I.

historicEvents.title: Elizabeth I Signs Death Warrant for Mary, Queen of Scots
historicEvents.description: Elizabeth I signed the death warrant for Mary, Queen of Scots, on February 1, 1587, as she was found guilty of plotting to assasinate Elizabeth. Mary, Queen of Scots, was beheaded at Fotheringhay Castle in England on February 8, 1587.
historicEvents.urlTitle: Elizabeth I.org - Mary Queen of Scots

historicEvents.title: The Honourable East India Company
historicEvents.description: The Honourable East India Company, a British joint-stock company, was established in 1600 for trade in the Indian Ocean region. At its height, the British East India Company had a private army which was twice the size of the British Army, ruled large sections of India, and revenues in the millions.
historicEvents.urlTitle: Historic UK - The East India Company

en.lifeSummary: When Edward Morris II was born in 1565, in Rycote, Oxfordshire, England, his father, Sir Knight Edward Morris, was 40 and his mother, Lady Margaret Williams of Kingscott, was 40. He had at least 2 sons with Lady Elizabeth Catherine Norreys. He died on 1 September 1603, in St Andrew, Holborn, Middlesex, England, at the age of 38, and was buried in St Andrew, Holborn, Middlesex, England.

ko.lifeSummary: Edward Morris II(이)가 1565년 영국 스퍼드실르 리컷에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Sir Knight Edward Morris(은)는 40세였고, 그의 어머니 Lady Margaret Williams of Kingscott(은)는 40세였다. 그는 Lady Elizabeth Catherine Norreys(와)과 적어도 두 명의 아들을 두었다. 그는 1603년 9월 1일 영국 미들레세스 홀본 스트 앤드루에서 38세의 나이로 사망했으며 영국 미들레세스 홀본 스트 앤드루에 묻혔다.
